steps.title: Reconcile the two studies
steps.content: Levine and Zervos found no enduring (long-lasting) increases in investment after liberalization. Henry found a significant increase specifically in the three years following liberalization. Together, these findings suggest investment rose briefly after liberalization but did not persist — a temporary boost that fades.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'liberalization may provide a boost to investment that fades over time.': Directly reconciles both studies: a short-term increase (Henry) with no lasting effect (Levine and Zervos). ✓
  • Choice B — 'economists' expectations about the effect of liberalization on investment were largely correct.': The passage never mentions what economists expected — this is unsupported. ✗
  • Choice C — 'companies typically do not benefit from liberalization until at least three years after liberalization occurs.': Misreads Henry's finding — the significant increase happened in the three years following liberalization, not after a delay. ✗
  • Choice D — 'investment growth is likely to be more consistent in countries that liberalize than in countries that do not.': Neither study compares liberalized to non-liberalized countries. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
